title: My Grief Has No Expiry Date
subtitle: An affirmation for when someone else's timeline for your grief feels like a verdict
excerpt: A single-sentence affirmation for the grief that has been going on too long according to everyone else. Rejects the premise that grief has a correct duration and gives the reader permission to be wherever they are.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on modern grief literature that rejects the stage model and the cultural pressure to resolve grief on a schedule.

practice.whenToUse: Use when someone has suggested you should be over it by now, or when you feel guilty for still grieving.

content.text: A short affirmation for the moment you are told, directly or indirectly, that you have been grieving too long. This is a counter-statement to the cultural clock.
Say it aloud. Three times. Slowly enough that you mean it each time.
My grief has no expiry date.

This is not a statement about staying in grief forever. It is a refusal of the idea that grief has a correct schedule. It may be six months. It may be six years. The duration does not say anything about your character or your love for them.

Written for homemade.education, drawing on contemporary grief writing that has moved away from the Kubler-Ross stage model toward a more truthful account of grief as non-linear, non-scheduled, and permanent in its own way even as it changes.
